data.title: Bitcoin is at $88,000 with liquidations at $195.55 million. Bitcoin ETFs had $175.3 million outflows. Ethereum ETFs had $52.7 million outflows.
data.description: Bitcoin has surged above $88,000 following a period of decline, with liquidations totaling $195.55 million in the last 24 hours. Bitcoin ETFs experienced net outflows of $175.3 million on Wednesday, while Ethereum ETFs saw net outflows of $52.7 million. According to Crypto Tony, Bitcoin's stagnant price movement suggests it is in an X wave phase, characterized by erratic and disruptive behavior. He predicts a potential decline, either immediately or after a brief rally, advising traders to be ready for both possibilities. CryptosBatman attributed the recent sideways trading to low holiday volume rather than inherent weakness in the market. Bitcoin is said to be consolidating in a classic pattern.

data.title: Article highlights the importance of trust in building strong relationships at work and home.
data.description: A high percentage of individuals aspire to establish strong, enduring connections in both their professional and personal lives. However, many are unsure of how to initiate the process of cultivating such relationships. Building trust is highlighted as a foundational element in fostering these powerful relationships, which are desired by a majority of people. This information underscores the significance of trust in interpersonal dynamics and its role in developing meaningful connections in the workplace and at home.

data.title: Dow Jones hits record close, S&P 500 at intraday high pre-Christmas. Nvidia seals major deal. Palantir, Tesla, Google in buy zones.
data.description: The Dow Jones achieved a record high closing, while the S&P 500 reached a new intraday peak just before the Christmas holiday. Stocks like Palantir, Tesla, Google, and Nvidia are currently attractive for purchase. However, Dow Jones futures saw a decline following Christmas, with Nvidia striking a significant deal in the market.

data.title: Sanofi buys Dynavax for $2.2 billion, expanding adult vaccine market with unique hepatitis B vaccine HEPLISAV-B.
data.description: Sanofi SA has announced its agreement to purchase Dynavax Technologies Corporation for $15.50 per share in cash, totaling about $2.2 billion. This acquisition will enhance Sanofi's position in adult immunization. One key asset is Dynavax's adult hepatitis B vaccine HEPLISAV-B, available in the U.S., known for its two-dose regimen that provides faster seroprotection compared to other vaccines requiring three doses over six months. The transaction also encompasses Dynavax's shingles vaccine candidate. This strategic move aims to bolster Sanofi's vaccine portfolio.

data.title: Citius Pharmaceuticals (CTXR) shares surged 22.12% after hours to $1.27, following launch of its oncology therapy LYMPHIR.
data.description: In after-hours trading, Citius Pharmaceuticals Inc. (NASDAQ: CTXR) witnessed a significant 22.12% increase, with shares rising to $1.27 on Tuesday. This surge followed the company's fiscal year 2025 business update and the official launch of its leading oncology therapy, LYMPHIR. The New Jersey-based biopharmaceutical firm disclosed that LYMPHIR (denileukin diftitox-cxdl), a recombinant engineered fusion protein, has been commercially released through its subsidiary Citius Oncology (NASDAQ: CTOR) as of December. Approved by the Food and Drug Administration, this therapy caters to adult patients with relapsed or refractory Stage I-III cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (CTCL) who have undergone a prior systemic therapy. Leonard Mazur, Chairman and CEO of Citius Pharmaceuticals, expressed in the company's Tuesday press release, "This achievement showcases our ability to execute..." Visit Benzinga.com for the complete story and keep track of the current price of CTXR stock.

data.title: Kate Hudson is defying expectations with her role as a blue-collar mom. Could it land her an Oscar nomination? #KateHudson #Oscars2022
data.description: After being typecast for years as a rom-com actress, her portrayal of a working-class mother who adores Neil Diamond could potentially lead her back to the Oscars.

data.title: Investors caution on 'Rot in Private Equity' as firms resort to circular deals to return cash amid difficulty selling companies.
data.description: Private equity firms have faced challenges in divesting the companies they own. As a solution, they have resorted to a tactic of selling these companies back to themselves in order to return cash to their clients. This alternative method has been employed due to difficulties in traditional exits in the current market environment.

data.title: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent seeks Fed Chair to reduce bank's role, end QE. Criticizes Fed for exacerbating wealth gap.
data.description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent is actively seeking a new Federal Reserve Chair committed to shrinking the central bank's footprint and ending quantitative easing. In an economic review podcast, Bessent criticized the Fed's "gain of function" experiments since 2008, arguing for a rollback to restore economic fairness. He labeled the Fed as the "engine of inequality," stating its policies increased the wealth gap by benefiting asset holders over others. Bessent criticized the Fed's low interest rates and asset purchases, which inflated the portfolios of the wealthy while neglecting Main Street. Full story on Benzinga.com.

data.title: Deutsche Bank warns of massive AI spending with no guaranteed return behind robust GDP data, highlights $4 trillion AI investments.
data.description: Deutsche Bank analysts have expressed concerns about the crucial role of AI investments in the latest US GDP data. The bank's analysts, Adrian Cox and Stefan Abrudan, highlighted the significance of tech-related spending in sustaining economic growth. They stated that without investments in AI-related sectors, the US would be close to a recession this year. Deutsche Bank's note also mentioned that hyperscalers are projected to spend $4 trillion on AI data centers by 2030, exceeding the inflation-adjusted cost of the US government's moon-landing program in the 1960s. This underscores the importance of AI spending in maintaining the country's economic stability and growth.

data.title: Judge dismisses Chamber of Commerce's lawsuit against Trump's $100,000 H-1B visa fee, citing president's broad authority.
data.description: A federal judge in Washington, D.C., Beryl Howell, has rejected the challenge from the U.S. Chamber of Commerce against the $100,000 fee imposed by President Donald Trump on new H-1B visas for highly skilled foreign workers. Judge Howell dismissed the Chamber of Commerce's argument that the fee went against federal immigration law and would lead to job cuts and reduced services by many companies, hospitals, and employers. The judge ruled that the fee was within the president's authority to regulate immigration. This decision disregards the claims made by the Chamber of Commerce, the largest business lobby group in the U.S., and highlights the continuous legal battles over immigration policies in the country. The ruling signifies a win for the Trump administration's efforts to restrict immigration and regulate H-1B visas.

data.title: Rosenberg disputes Q3 GDP growth of 4.3% as he calculates real growth at 0.8%, citing government spending and depleted savings.
data.description: The U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis reported a 4.3% increase in third-quarter real GDP, but economist David Rosenberg argues the number is misleading. He believes the actual growth rate is around 0.8%, after accounting for factors like government spending and depleted savings. Despite official figures showing gains in consumer spending, exports, and government spending, Rosenberg claims these numbers are inflated. He points to stagnant personal disposable income growth as evidence that the economy is not as strong as it appears. Rosenberg alleges that the GDP report is manipulated, similar to the way CPI data is often questioned. This suggests that the economy may not be growing as robustly as recent data indicates.

data.title: Treasury Secretary Bessent confirms search for new Fed Chair to shrink institution, end perpetual QE era. Fed criticized as "engine of inequality".
data.description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent is actively interviewing candidates for the next Federal Reserve Chair, looking for a leader committed to reducing the central bank's influence and stopping perpetual quantitative easing. In a podcast, Bessent criticized the Fed's monetary policies of the past 15 years, particularly large-scale asset purchases that began in 2008. He called the Fed "the engine of inequality" for widening the wealth gap, stating it shouldn't exacerbate inequalities. Bessent argued that by keeping interest rates low and buying trillions in assets, the Fed benefited the wealthy while neglecting the general population. This shift in policy aims to create economic fairness by recalibrating the Fed's actions.

data.title: Sen. Warren warns Trump's Fed nominee will be a 'puppet,' cites Trump's loyalty vow. She raises alarm over president's intent for Fed Chair; warns of prioritizing loyalty over monetary independence.
data.description: Senator Elizabeth Warren of Massachusetts is raising concerns in response to President Donald Trump's recent comments about his choice for the next Chair of the Federal Reserve after Jerome Powell steps down in May 2026. Trump's statement, made on Truth Social, stated, "Anybody that disagrees with me will never be the Fed Chairman," which Warren believes indicates a prioritization of loyalty over monetary independence in future nominees. She expressed that Trump's declaration makes it apparent that he intends to appoint a compliant individual who will cater to his own agenda rather than the interests of the American public. Warren emphasized the significance of this move and believes that the nominee will essentially be a "puppet" under Trump's control. For further details, refer to the complete article on Benzinga.com.

data.title: Treasury Sec Bessent states private economy booming under Trump, with GDP growth up 4.7%, contrast to Biden's led by govt spending.
data.description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent recently remarked on the significant acceleration of the U.S. private economy during President Donald Trump's term, highlighting a robust growth in real economic output exclusive of government involvement. Specifically, Bessent emphasized that the private economy has been flourishing under Trump, with GDP growth, excluding government activity, surging by 4.7% at an annualized rate over the previous two quarters. He contrasted this with the administration under Biden, noting that previous growth was heavily reliant on government spending, leading to notable inflation and a decline in living standards. This positive trend in the private economy indicates a stark deviation from previous economic dynamics.
